graph.label: Fourier Analysis
design_notes: Curated conceptual knowledge graph of Fourier analysis (not tied to a single text).
Nodes are named concepts/results; edges form a prerequisite DAG via `requires`/`instance_of`.
Soft associations live in each node's `related[]` (panel-only, not layout edges).

domains.label: Foundations & Function Spaces
domains.description: Periodic functions, inner-product geometry, and the function spaces (L^1, L^2, Schwartz) where Fourier analysis lives.

domains.label: Fourier Series
domains.description: Decomposing periodic functions into harmonics: coefficients, partial sums, and the basic identities.

domains.label: Convergence & Summability
domains.description: When and how Fourier series converge: kernels, summability methods, and the classical convergence theorems.

domains.label: The Fourier Transform
domains.description: Fourier analysis on the line and R^d: inversion, convolution, Plancherel, and the uncertainty principle.

domains.label: Distributions
domains.description: Generalized functions: tempered distributions, the Dirac delta, and Fourier analysis beyond classical functions.

domains.label: Discrete & Computational
domains.description: Finite and sampled Fourier analysis: the DFT, the FFT, and the sampling theorem.

domains.label: Applications
domains.description: Where it all pays off: PDEs, signal processing, number theory, and probability.

items.label: Periodic Function
items.statement: A function repeating after a fixed period; equivalently a function on the circle.
items.formal_statement: $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{C}$ is periodic of period $T$ if $f(x+T)=f(x)$ for all $x$. We work with $2\pi$-periodic functions, i.e. functions on the circle $\mathbb{T}=\mathbb{R}/2\pi\mathbb{Z}$.
items.intuition: The natural domain of Fourier series: gluing the ends of an interval into a loop.

items.label: Complex Exponential
items.statement: The pure harmonics $e^{inx}$ that serve as the building blocks of Fourier analysis.
items.formal_statement: $e_n(x)=e^{inx}$, $n\in\mathbb{Z}$. By Euler's formula $e^{inx}=\cos nx+i\sin nx$.
items.intuition: Each $e_n$ is a single frequency; Fourier analysis writes every function as a superposition of these.

items.label: Inner Product Space
items.statement: A vector space with an inner product, giving lengths and angles.
items.formal_statement: A complex vector space $V$ with $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle:V\times V\to\mathbb{C}$ that is linear, conjugate-symmetric, and positive-definite. For functions, $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f\overline{g}\,dx$.
items.intuition: Provides the geometry — orthogonality and projection — behind Fourier coefficients.

items.label: Orthonormal System
items.statement: A family of mutually orthogonal unit vectors; the exponentials $e^{inx}$ form one.
items.formal_statement: $\{\varphi_k\}$ with $\langle\varphi_j,\varphi_k\rangle=\delta_{jk}$. The system $\{e^{inx}\}_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}$ is orthonormal on $\mathbb{T}$.
items.intuition: An orthonormal basis lets you read off coordinates by projection — exactly what a Fourier coefficient is.

items.label: L^p Space
items.statement: Functions whose $p$-th power is integrable, normed by the $L^p$ norm.
items.formal_statement: $L^p(\mathbb{T})=\{f:\|f\|_p=(\frac{1}{2\pi}\int|f|^p)^{1/p}<\infty\}$, $1\le p\le\infty$.
items.intuition: The scale of spaces in which different convergence results live; $L^1$ and $L^2$ are the key cases.

items.label: L^1 (Integrable Functions)
items.statement: Absolutely integrable functions — the minimal home where Fourier coefficients exist.
items.formal_statement: $L^1(\mathbb{T})=\{f:\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f|\,dx<\infty\}$.
items.intuition: Integrability is all you need to define $\hat f(n)$; convergence questions need more.

items.label: L^2 (Square-Integrable)
items.statement: The Hilbert space of finite-energy functions, where Fourier theory is cleanest.
items.formal_statement: $L^2(\mathbb{T})$ with $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int f\overline g$ is a Hilbert space, and $\{e^{inx}\}$ is an orthonormal basis.
items.intuition: Square-integrability turns Fourier analysis into orthogonal geometry: Parseval and mean-square convergence hold here.

items.label: Hilbert Space
items.statement: A complete inner-product space — the abstract setting that makes $L^2$ work.
items.formal_statement: An inner-product space that is complete in the induced norm. $L^2(\mathbb{T})$ and $\ell^2(\mathbb{Z})$ are the central examples.
items.intuition: Completeness guarantees that orthogonal expansions actually converge to a limit in the space.

items.label: Schwartz Space
items.statement: Smooth functions that, with all derivatives, decay faster than any polynomial.
items.formal_statement: $\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R})=\{f\in C^\infty:\sup_x|x^\alpha\partial^\beta f|<\infty\ \forall\alpha,\beta\}$.
items.intuition: The ideal playground for the Fourier transform: it maps $\mathcal S$ to itself bijectively.

items.label: Convolution
items.statement: An averaging product of two functions that becomes multiplication after Fourier transform.
items.formal_statement: $(f*g)(x)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(y)g(x-y)\,dy$ (circle); on $\mathbb{R}$, $(f*g)(x)=\int f(y)g(x-y)\,dy$.
items.intuition: Smoothing by a kernel; the operation Fourier analysis is built to diagonalize.

items.label: Fourier Coefficient
items.statement: The amount of frequency $n$ present in $f$, obtained by projecting onto $e^{inx}$.
items.formal_statement: $\hat f(n)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)e^{-inx}\,dx=\langle f,e_n\rangle$.
items.intuition: The coordinate of $f$ along the $n$-th harmonic — a single complex number per frequency.

items.label: Fourier Series
items.statement: The infinite sum of harmonics reconstructing a function from its coefficients.
items.formal_statement: $f(x)\sim\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\hat f(n)e^{inx}$.
items.intuition: The candidate reconstruction of $f$; whether it equals $f$ is the convergence question.

items.label: Trigonometric Polynomial
items.statement: A finite sum of harmonics; the dense, well-behaved functions used for approximation.
items.formal_statement: $p(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N} c_n e^{inx}$.
items.intuition: Finite Fourier series — the simple objects everything is approximated by.

items.label: Partial Sum
items.statement: The truncation of a Fourier series to frequencies $|n|\le N$.
items.formal_statement: $S_N f(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N}\hat f(n)e^{inx}=(D_N*f)(x)$, where $D_N$ is the Dirichlet kernel.
items.intuition: The finite approximation whose limit (if any) defines convergence of the series.

items.label: Bessel's Inequality
items.statement: The energy of the Fourier coefficients never exceeds the energy of the function.
items.formal_statement: $\sum_{n}|\hat f(n)|^2\le\|f\|_2^2$ for $f\in L^2(\mathbb{T})$.
items.intuition: Projections can only lose energy; equality (Parseval) signals a complete basis.

items.label: Parseval's Identity
items.statement: Energy is exactly conserved between a function and its Fourier coefficients.
items.formal_statement: $\sum_{n}|\hat f(n)|^2=\|f\|_2^2$, and $\langle f,g\rangle=\sum_n\hat f(n)\overline{\hat g(n)}$, for $f,g\in L^2(\mathbb{T})$.
items.intuition: Bessel becomes equality because $\{e^{inx}\}$ is a complete orthonormal basis — Fourier is an isometry.

items.label: Riemann–Lebesgue Lemma
items.statement: Fourier coefficients of an integrable function vanish at high frequency.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in L^1(\mathbb{T})$ then $\hat f(n)\to 0$ as $|n|\to\infty$.
items.intuition: Rapid oscillation averages out: no integrable signal has energy concentrated at infinitely high frequency.

items.label: Uniqueness Theorem
items.statement: A function is determined by its Fourier coefficients.
items.formal_statement: If $f$ is continuous on $\mathbb{T}$ and $\hat f(n)=0$ for all $n$, then $f\equiv 0$.
items.intuition: The Fourier transform loses no information — distinct functions have distinct spectra.

items.label: Best Approximation Property
items.statement: Among all trig polynomials of degree $N$, the partial sum is the closest in $L^2$.
items.formal_statement: $\|f-S_N f\|_2=\min\{\|f-p\|_2:\deg p\le N\}$; the error is orthogonal to the span of $\{e^{inx}\}_{|n|\le N}$.
items.intuition: Truncating the Fourier series is exactly orthogonal projection onto the low frequencies.

items.label: Decay–Smoothness Duality
items.statement: The smoother a function, the faster its Fourier coefficients decay.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in C^k(\mathbb{T})$ then $\hat f(n)=O(|n|^{-k})$; conversely fast decay forces smoothness.
items.intuition: Regularity in $x$ and decay in frequency are two sides of one coin — a recurring theme.

items.label: Good Kernel (Approximate Identity)
items.statement: A family of kernels concentrating at the origin, so convolution recovers $f$.
items.formal_statement: $\{K_n\}$ with $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int K_n=1$, $\int|K_n|\le M$, and $\int_{|x|>\delta}|K_n|\to0$. Then $K_n*f\to f$.
items.intuition: A sequence of smoothing bumps shrinking to a spike: convolving with them undoes the smoothing in the limit.

items.label: Dirichlet Kernel
items.statement: The kernel of the partial-sum operator — and the source of convergence trouble.
items.formal_statement: $D_N(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N}e^{inx}=\dfrac{\sin((N+\tfrac12)x)}{\sin(x/2)}$, so $S_N f=D_N*f$.
items.intuition: Not a good kernel: $\|D_N\|_1\sim\log N$ grows, which is why pointwise convergence can fail.

items.label: Fejér Kernel
items.statement: The Cesàro average of Dirichlet kernels — a genuine good kernel.
items.formal_statement: $F_N(x)=\frac{1}{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}D_n(x)=\frac{1}{N+1}\left(\frac{\sin(\frac{N+1}{2}x)}{\sin(x/2)}\right)^2\ge0$.
items.intuition: Averaging tames the oscillation of $D_N$; non-negativity makes it an approximate identity.

items.label: Poisson Kernel
items.statement: The good kernel behind Abel summation and harmonic extension to the disk.
items.formal_statement: $P_r(\theta)=\sum_{n}r^{|n|}e^{in\theta}=\dfrac{1-r^2}{1-2r\cos\theta+r^2}$, $0\le r<1$.
items.intuition: Solves the Dirichlet problem on the disk; as $r\to1$ it recovers boundary data.

items.label: Cesàro Summation
items.statement: Averaging partial sums to coax convergence out of a divergent series.
items.formal_statement: $\sigma_N f=\frac{1}{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}S_n f=F_N*f$.
items.intuition: A gentler limit: many series that diverge converge once you average their partial sums.

items.label: Abel Summation
items.statement: Summation by radial limits $r\to1$, smoother still than Cesàro.
items.formal_statement: $A_r f=\sum_n r^{|n|}\hat f(n)e^{inx}=P_r*f$.
items.intuition: Weights high frequencies by $r^{|n|}<1$, then lets $r\to1$ — connects series to the disk.

items.label: Fejér's Theorem
items.statement: Cesàro means of the Fourier series of a continuous function converge uniformly.
items.formal_statement: If $f$ is continuous on $\mathbb{T}$, then $\sigma_N f=F_N*f\to f$ uniformly.
items.intuition: Even when the series itself misbehaves, its averages always reconstruct a continuous $f$ — and prove Weierstrass approximation.

items.label: Mean-Square Convergence
items.statement: Fourier series converge in $L^2$ for every square-integrable function.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in L^2(\mathbb{T})$, $\|f-S_N f\|_2\to 0$.
items.intuition: In the energy norm there is no subtlety: the series always converges — a direct consequence of completeness.

items.label: Pointwise Convergence Problem
items.statement: Whether $S_N f(x)\to f(x)$ at a point — delicate and not automatic.
items.formal_statement: $S_N f(x_0)\to f(x_0)$? True under regularity hypotheses; can fail for merely continuous $f$.
items.intuition: The hardest classical question: convergence at individual points needs local smoothness.

items.label: Dini's Criterion
items.statement: A local integrability condition guaranteeing pointwise convergence.
items.formal_statement: If $\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\left|\frac{f(x_0+t)-f(x_0)}{t}\right|dt<\infty$, then $S_N f(x_0)\to f(x_0)$.
items.intuition: Mild Hölder-type regularity at a point is enough to force the series to converge there.

items.label: Dirichlet's Convergence Theorem
items.statement: Piecewise-smooth functions converge to the midpoint of their jumps.
items.formal_statement: If $f$ is piecewise $C^1$, then $S_N f(x)\to\tfrac12\big(f(x^+)+f(x^-)\big)$.
items.intuition: The classical sufficient condition: nice enough functions always converge, jumps splitting the difference.

items.label: Gibbs Phenomenon
items.statement: Persistent ~9% overshoot of partial sums near a jump discontinuity.
items.formal_statement: Near a jump, $S_N f$ overshoots by a fixed fraction ($\approx 0.0895$ of the jump) that does not vanish as $N\to\infty$.
items.intuition: The ringing you see truncating a square wave: the overshoot narrows but never shrinks.

items.label: Carleson's Theorem
items.statement: Fourier series of $L^2$ functions converge almost everywhere.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in L^2(\mathbb{T})$, $S_N f(x)\to f(x)$ for almost every $x$.
items.intuition: The deep resolution of a 150-year-old question: a.e. pointwise convergence holds in $L^2$.

items.label: Du Bois-Reymond Counterexample
items.statement: A continuous function whose Fourier series diverges at a point.
items.formal_statement: There exists $f\in C(\mathbb{T})$ with $\limsup_N|S_N f(0)|=\infty$.
items.intuition: Continuity alone does not guarantee pointwise convergence — the reason Fejér/Cesàro averaging matters.

items.label: Fourier Transform
items.statement: The continuous-frequency analogue of Fourier coefficients, for functions on $\mathbb{R}$.
items.formal_statement: $\hat f(\xi)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx$.
items.intuition: Replaces the discrete spectrum of a periodic function with a continuous one on the whole line.

items.label: Fourier Inversion Theorem
items.statement: The transform is invertible: $f$ is rebuilt from $\hat f$ by the dual integral.
items.formal_statement: For $f\in\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R})$, $f(x)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi$.
items.intuition: Analysis and synthesis are symmetric — the inverse transform is the transform with a sign flip.

items.label: Convolution Theorem
items.statement: The Fourier transform turns convolution into pointwise multiplication.
items.formal_statement: $\widehat{f*g}=\hat f\cdot\hat g$, and dually $\widehat{fg}=\hat f*\hat g$.
items.intuition: Why Fourier methods work: hard convolution integrals become easy products in frequency.

items.label: Plancherel's Theorem
items.statement: The Fourier transform is a unitary map on $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ — energy is preserved.
items.formal_statement: $\|\hat f\|_{L^2}=\|f\|_{L^2}$, and the transform extends to a unitary operator on $L^2(\mathbb{R})$.
items.intuition: The continuous counterpart of Parseval: Fourier is a rotation of the infinite-dimensional space $L^2$.

items.label: Gaussian (Self-Dual)
items.statement: The Gaussian is its own Fourier transform — the fixed point of the transform.
items.formal_statement: If $f(x)=e^{-\pi x^2}$ then $\hat f(\xi)=e^{-\pi\xi^2}$.
items.intuition: The extremal, perfectly balanced function: minimal uncertainty, invariant under Fourier.

items.label: Uncertainty Principle
items.statement: A function and its transform cannot both be sharply localized.
items.formal_statement: $\left(\int x^2|f|^2\right)\left(\int \xi^2|\hat f|^2\right)\ge\frac{\|f\|_2^4}{16\pi^2}$, with equality for Gaussians.
items.intuition: Concentration in time forces spread in frequency — the mathematics behind Heisenberg's principle.

items.label: Poisson Summation Formula
items.statement: Summing a function over a lattice equals summing its transform over the dual lattice.
items.formal_statement: $\sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}f(n)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}\hat f(n)$ (for nice $f$).
items.intuition: The bridge between the Fourier transform and Fourier series — periodization in $x$ is sampling in $\xi$.

items.label: Fourier Transform on R^d
items.statement: The multivariable transform, built from the one-dimensional one coordinatewise.
items.formal_statement: $\hat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^d} f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\cdot\xi}\,dx$, $x,\xi\in\mathbb{R}^d$.
items.intuition: Same theory in higher dimensions; rotation-invariance makes radial functions especially clean.

items.label: Differentiation Rule
items.statement: Differentiation becomes multiplication by frequency under the transform.
items.formal_statement: $\widehat{f'}(\xi)=2\pi i\xi\,\hat f(\xi)$ and $\widehat{xf}(\xi)=\frac{-1}{2\pi i}\frac{d}{d\xi}\hat f(\xi)$.
items.intuition: Turns differential operators into algebra — the key to solving PDEs by Fourier methods.

items.label: Tempered Distribution
items.statement: A continuous linear functional on Schwartz space — a generalized function.
items.formal_statement: $T\in\mathcal{S}'(\mathbb{R})$, the continuous dual of $\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R})$; functions of moderate growth embed via $T_f(\varphi)=\int f\varphi$.
items.intuition: Lets you Fourier-transform objects (like $1$, $\delta$, polynomials) that aren't integrable.

items.label: Dirac Delta
items.statement: The idealized unit spike: evaluation at a point, the identity for convolution.
items.formal_statement: $\delta(\varphi)=\varphi(0)$; $f*\delta=f$ and $\hat\delta\equiv 1$.
items.intuition: Not a function but a distribution — the limit of any approximate identity.

items.label: Weak Derivative
items.statement: Differentiation defined by moving the derivative onto the test function.
items.formal_statement: $T'(\varphi)=-T(\varphi')$, extending classical differentiation to all distributions.
items.intuition: Every distribution is infinitely differentiable — discontinuities and spikes included.

items.label: Fourier Transform of Distributions
items.statement: The transform extends to all tempered distributions by duality.
items.formal_statement: $\hat T(\varphi)=T(\hat\varphi)$ for $\varphi\in\mathcal{S}$; consistent with the classical transform.
items.intuition: Gives meaning to $\hat 1=\delta$ and the transform of every polynomially-bounded object.

items.label: Roots of Unity
items.statement: The $N$ equally-spaced points on the unit circle — discrete harmonics.
items.formal_statement: $\omega_N=e^{2\pi i/N}$; the characters of $\mathbb{Z}/N\mathbb{Z}$ are $k\mapsto\omega_N^{jk}$.
items.intuition: The finite analogue of $e^{inx}$: the building blocks of the discrete transform.

items.label: Fourier Analysis on Z(N)
items.statement: Fourier theory on the cyclic group, where everything is a finite sum.
items.formal_statement: Functions on $\mathbb{Z}/N\mathbb{Z}$ expand in the orthogonal characters $\{\omega_N^{jk}\}$; the cleanest, fully finite Fourier theory.
items.intuition: A finite-dimensional model where convergence is trivial and structure is transparent.

items.label: Discrete Fourier Transform
items.statement: The Fourier transform of a length-$N$ sample vector.
items.formal_statement: $\hat a(k)=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1} a_j\,\omega_N^{-jk}$, $\omega_N=e^{2\pi i/N}$.
items.intuition: What a computer actually evaluates: the finite, exact Fourier transform of sampled data.

items.label: Discrete-Time Fourier Transform
items.statement: The transform of an infinite sequence — a periodic function of frequency.
items.formal_statement: $\hat a(\xi)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}} a_n e^{-2\pi i n\xi}$, periodic in $\xi$.
items.intuition: The dual of Fourier series: discrete in time, continuous and periodic in frequency.

items.label: Fast Fourier Transform
items.statement: An $O(N\log N)$ algorithm computing the DFT by recursive splitting.
items.formal_statement: Cooley–Tukey factorizes the DFT of size $N$ into two of size $N/2$, giving $O(N\log N)$ vs.\ $O(N^2)$.
items.intuition: The algorithm that made Fourier methods computationally ubiquitous.

items.label: Circular Convolution
items.statement: Convolution on $\mathbb{Z}/N\mathbb{Z}$, diagonalized by the DFT.
items.formal_statement: $(a\circledast b)_k=\sum_j a_j b_{k-j\bmod N}$, and $\widehat{a\circledast b}=\hat a\cdot\hat b$.
items.intuition: The discrete convolution theorem — why fast filtering goes through the FFT.

items.label: Nyquist–Shannon Sampling
items.statement: A band-limited signal is perfectly reconstructed from samples at twice its bandwidth.
items.formal_statement: If $\hat f$ is supported in $[-W,W]$, then $f(x)=\sum_n f\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2W}\right)\mathrm{sinc}(2Wx-n)$.
items.intuition: Bridges continuous and discrete: sampling fast enough loses nothing.

items.label: Aliasing
items.statement: Undersampling makes high frequencies masquerade as low ones.
items.formal_statement: Sampling below the Nyquist rate folds frequencies outside $[-f_s/2,f_s/2]$ back into that band.
items.intuition: The failure mode of sampling — why anti-alias filtering precedes digitization.

items.label: Heat Equation
items.statement: Fourier's original problem: diffusion solved by decaying harmonics.
items.formal_statement: $u_t=u_{xx}$ with $u(x,0)=f(x)$ is solved by $u(x,t)=\sum_n\hat f(n)e^{-n^2 t}e^{inx}$.
items.intuition: Each harmonic decays at its own rate $e^{-n^2 t}$ — the problem that launched Fourier analysis.

items.label: Wave Equation
items.statement: Vibrating strings as superpositions of standing harmonic waves.
items.formal_statement: $u_{tt}=u_{xx}$ solved by $u(x,t)=\sum_n\big(a_n\cos nt+b_n\sin nt\big)e^{inx}$.
items.intuition: The physical origin of harmonics — d'Alembert and Bernoulli's standing waves.

items.label: Isoperimetric Inequality
items.statement: Among curves of fixed length, the circle encloses the most area — via Parseval.
items.formal_statement: For a simple closed curve of length $L$ enclosing area $A$: $A\le\frac{L^2}{4\pi}$, equality iff a circle.
items.intuition: A geometry theorem proved by expanding the boundary curve in a Fourier series.

items.label: Weyl Equidistribution
items.statement: Irrational rotations fill the circle uniformly — detected by Fourier coefficients.
items.formal_statement: $\{n\gamma\bmod 1\}$ is equidistributed iff $\frac1N\sum_{n\le N}e^{2\pi i k n\gamma}\to0$ for all $k\ne0$ (Weyl's criterion).
items.intuition: A number-theory equidistribution test reduced to the decay of exponential sums.

items.label: Central Limit Theorem (Fourier proof)
items.statement: Sums of independent variables become Gaussian — convolution of densities seen in frequency.
items.formal_statement: Characteristic functions $\varphi(\xi)=\hat\mu(\xi)$ multiply under convolution; $\varphi^n$ converges to the Gaussian transform $e^{-\xi^2/2}$.
items.intuition: The Fourier transform diagonalizes convolution, turning the CLT into a limit of products.

items.label: Filtering & Signal Processing
items.statement: Designing systems that pass or reject frequency bands via multiplication of spectra.
items.formal_statement: A linear time-invariant filter acts by $\widehat{(h*x)}=\hat h\cdot\hat x$; the transfer function $\hat h$ shapes the spectrum.
items.intuition: All of audio/image processing: convolve in time = multiply in frequency, computed by the FFT.

items.label: Primes in Arithmetic Progressions
items.statement: Infinitely many primes in each valid progression — proved with Fourier analysis on $(\mathbb{Z}/q)^\times$.
items.formal_statement: If $\gcd(a,q)=1$ there are infinitely many primes $\equiv a\pmod q$; the proof expands an indicator in Dirichlet characters and uses $L(1,\chi)\ne0$.
items.intuition: Finite Fourier analysis on a group of units, combined with $L$-functions, cracks a number-theory landmark.

items.label: Lebesgue Integral
items.statement: The integral that makes the Fourier function spaces complete.
items.formal_statement: $\int f\,d\mu$ defined for measurable $f$; limits behave well (unlike the Riemann integral), making $L^p$ complete.
items.intuition: The right notion of integration — without it $L^1$ and $L^2$ would have holes.

items.label: Almost Everywhere
items.statement: A property holding except on a set of measure zero.
items.formal_statement: $P$ holds a.e. if $\mu(\{x:\neg P(x)\})=0$. Functions equal a.e. are identified in $L^p$.
items.intuition: The equivalence that lets us treat $L^p$ elements as functions; the natural mode of Fourier convergence (cf. Carleson).

items.label: Dominated Convergence
items.statement: Interchange of limit and integral under a dominating function.
items.formal_statement: If $f_n\to f$ a.e. and $|f_n|\le g\in L^1$, then $\int f_n\to\int f$.
items.intuition: The workhorse that justifies swapping limits, sums, and integrals throughout Fourier analysis.

items.label: Fubini–Tonelli Theorem
items.statement: Iterated integrals may be swapped for (absolutely) integrable functions.
items.formal_statement: $\iint f(x,y)\,dx\,dy=\iint f(x,y)\,dy\,dx$ when $f\in L^1$ (Fubini) or $f\ge0$ (Tonelli).
items.intuition: What makes convolution and the inversion formula well-defined — you reorder double integrals.

items.label: Real (Sine–Cosine) Series
items.statement: The classical $a_n\cos+b_n\sin$ form, equivalent to the complex series.
items.formal_statement: $f(x)\sim\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_{n\ge1}(a_n\cos nx+b_n\sin nx)$, with $a_n=\frac1\pi\int f\cos nx$, $b_n=\frac1\pi\int f\sin nx$.
items.intuition: The original 19th-century form; real and imaginary parts of the complex coefficients.

items.label: Half-Range Expansions
items.statement: Expanding a function on $[0,L]$ in pure sines or pure cosines via even/odd extension.
items.formal_statement: Extend $f$ on $[0,\pi]$ evenly (cosine series) or oddly (sine series) to $[-\pi,\pi]$.
items.intuition: How boundary conditions pick sine vs. cosine series when solving PDEs on an interval.

items.label: Riemann Localization Principle
items.statement: Convergence of a Fourier series at a point depends only on $f$ near that point.
items.formal_statement: If $f=g$ on a neighborhood of $x_0$, then $S_N f(x_0)$ and $S_N g(x_0)$ converge or diverge together.
items.intuition: A striking fact: even though coefficients are global integrals, pointwise convergence is purely local.

items.label: Term-by-Term Differentiation
items.statement: When the differentiated series still converges to the derivative.
items.formal_statement: If $\sum |n\,\hat f(n)|<\infty$, then $f'(x)=\sum_n in\,\hat f(n)e^{inx}$.
items.intuition: Smoothness (fast coefficient decay) is exactly what licenses differentiating a series term by term.

items.label: Weierstrass Approximation
items.statement: Every continuous periodic function is a uniform limit of trigonometric polynomials.
items.formal_statement: Trigonometric polynomials are dense in $C(\mathbb{T})$ — an immediate corollary of Fejér's theorem.
items.intuition: The density result underpinning completeness of the exponential basis; Fejér's payoff.

items.label: Abel's Theorem
items.statement: A convergent series is Abel summable to the same value — summability is consistent.
items.formal_statement: If $\sum a_n=s$ converges, then $\lim_{r\to1^-}\sum a_n r^n=s$.
items.intuition: Guarantees the gentler summation methods don't change answers where ordinary convergence already holds.

items.label: Translation & Modulation
items.statement: Shifting in time is modulation in frequency, and vice versa.
items.formal_statement: $\widehat{f(\cdot-a)}(\xi)=e^{-2\pi ia\xi}\hat f(\xi)$ and $\widehat{e^{2\pi ibx}f}(\xi)=\hat f(\xi-b)$.
items.intuition: The symmetry making the transform translation-covariant — the basis of time-frequency analysis.

items.label: Transform of L^1 is Continuous & Decays
items.statement: The Fourier transform of an integrable function is continuous and vanishes at infinity.
items.formal_statement: If $f\in L^1(\mathbb{R})$ then $\hat f\in C_0(\mathbb{R})$: continuous with $\hat f(\xi)\to0$ as $|\xi|\to\infty$.
items.intuition: The Riemann–Lebesgue lemma on the line — integrability buys smoothness and decay of the spectrum.

items.label: Band-Limited Function
items.statement: A function whose Fourier transform vanishes outside a bounded frequency band.
items.formal_statement: $\hat f$ supported in $[-W,W]$; equivalently $f$ contains no frequencies above $W$.
items.intuition: The signals that sampling reconstructs exactly — finite bandwidth, infinite smoothness.

items.label: Paley–Wiener Theorem
items.statement: Band-limited functions are exactly restrictions of entire functions of exponential type.
items.formal_statement: $f$ is band-limited to $[-W,W]$ iff $f$ extends to an entire function with $|f(z)|\le Ce^{2\pi W|z|}$.
items.intuition: Trades a support condition in frequency for a growth condition in the complex plane.

items.label: Heat Kernel on R
items.statement: The Gaussian semigroup solving the heat equation on the whole line.
items.formal_statement: $H_t(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi t}}e^{-x^2/4t}$, with $\hat H_t(\xi)=e^{-4\pi^2 t\xi^2}$; $u=H_t*f$ solves $u_t=u_{xx}$.
items.intuition: A good kernel that is also a Gaussian — convolving with it smooths data and solves diffusion.

items.label: Test Function
items.statement: A smooth, compactly supported function used to probe distributions.
items.formal_statement: $\varphi\in C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$; the space $\mathcal{D}$ of test functions sits inside $\mathcal{S}$.
items.intuition: The 'measuring instruments' of distribution theory — you know a distribution by how it pairs with these.

items.label: Convolution of Distributions
items.statement: Extending convolution to distributions, with the delta as identity.
items.formal_statement: $(T*\varphi)(x)=T(\tau_x\tilde\varphi)$; $\delta*T=T$ and $\widehat{T*S}=\hat T\hat S$ under support conditions.
items.intuition: Lets you convolve spikes and generalized functions — essential for solving PDEs.

items.label: Fundamental Solution
items.statement: A distribution $E$ with $LE=\delta$, giving solutions by convolution.
items.formal_statement: For a constant-coefficient operator $L$, if $LE=\delta$ then $u=E*f$ solves $Lu=f$.
items.intuition: The Fourier transform finds $E$ by dividing by the symbol — the master template for linear PDEs.

items.label: Parseval for the DFT
items.statement: Energy is conserved by the discrete transform, up to a factor of $N$.
items.intuition: The DFT is (a scaling of) a unitary matrix — the finite Plancherel identity.

items.label: Discrete Cosine Transform
items.statement: A real, cosine-only transform of sampled data — the engine of JPEG/MP3.
items.formal_statement: $C_k=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1} a_j\cos\!\big[\tfrac{\pi}{N}(j+\tfrac12)k\big]$; a DFT of an even extension.
items.intuition: Concentrates energy in few coefficients, which is why it dominates lossy compression.

items.label: Separation of Variables
items.statement: Solving linear PDEs by expanding in eigenfunctions of the spatial operator.
items.formal_statement: Seek $u(x,t)=\sum_n c_n(t)e_n(x)$ where $e_n$ are eigenfunctions; the PDE decouples into ODEs for $c_n(t)$.
items.intuition: The technique that turns Fourier series into PDE solutions — Fourier's original method.

items.label: Dirichlet Problem on the Disk
items.statement: Harmonic extension of boundary data, solved by the Poisson kernel.
items.formal_statement: $\Delta u=0$ in the disk with $u=f$ on the boundary is solved by $u(r,\theta)=(P_r*f)(\theta)$.
items.intuition: Where the Poisson kernel earns its name — Abel summation is harmonic extension.

items.label: Theta Function & Functional Equation
items.statement: The identity $\theta(1/t)=\sqrt{t}\,\theta(t)$, straight from Poisson summation.
items.formal_statement: $\theta(t)=\sum_{n}e^{-\pi n^2 t}$ satisfies $\theta(1/t)=\sqrt{t}\,\theta(t)$, via Poisson summation on the Gaussian.
items.intuition: A bridge to number theory (the Riemann zeta functional equation) built on Poisson summation.

items.label: Free Schrödinger Equation
items.statement: Quantum free evolution diagonalized by the Fourier transform.
items.formal_statement: $i u_t=-u_{xx}$ solves via $\hat u(\xi,t)=e^{-4\pi^2 i\xi^2 t}\hat u_0(\xi)$ — multiplication in frequency.
items.intuition: Like the heat equation but with imaginary time: dispersion instead of diffusion.

items.label: Fraunhofer Diffraction
items.statement: The far-field light pattern of an aperture is the Fourier transform of its shape.
items.formal_statement: Far-field amplitude $\propto\hat{\mathbb{1}_A}(\xi)$, the Fourier transform of the aperture indicator.
items.intuition: Optics computes Fourier transforms physically — a slit's pattern is a sinc, a circle's an Airy disk.
